Tutorial: Week 8 — Midterm Review and Block Cross-Filling ​

For: Teaching Assistants conducting tutorial sessions (Apr 23, 2026) Related Lectures: Lecture 8: Cross-Filling Projections, Lecture 10: Constructing Projections, Lecture 12: Determinant Duration: 50 minutes Context: This is the first tutorial after the midterm exam. Part A reviews two conceptually challenging True/False questions from the exam. Part B introduces block cross-filling — a unifying idea that appeared in Lectures 10 and 12 but was never isolated as a standalone method. Students should leave understanding: (1) how projection theory connects rank and trace, (2) how to cross-fill block matrices by treating a submatrix as the "pivot block."

Part A: Midterm Review — Two Key Ideas (25 min) ​

Problem 1: If AB=In, does switching the order preserve rank? (12 min) ​

True or False: If AB=In, then rank(BA)=n.

(a) Start by asking: what can we say about BA? Compute (BA)2.

Solution(BA)2=B(AB)A=B⋅In⋅A=BA

So BA is a projection (P2=P).

(b) Now use the projection theory from Lecture 8: for any projection P, what is the relationship between rank(P) and tr(P)?

Solution

From Lecture 8: if P2=P, then rank(P)=tr(P).

Quick reminder why: Cross-fill P=R1+⋯+Rr. Each Ri is a rank-1 projection, so tr(Ri)=1. Therefore tr(P)=r=rank(P).

(c) Combine (a) and (b) to show rank(BA)=n.

Solution

Since BA is a projection: rank(BA)=tr(BA).

Now use the trace-cycling property:

tr(BA)=tr(AB)=tr(In)=n

Therefore rank(BA)=n. ◻

Attention

The Power of This Argument

Three ingredients combine in one line:

  1. AB=I⇒(BA)2=BA — algebraic manipulation
  2. Projection ⇒ rank = trace — the Lecture 8 theorem
  3. Trace cycling: tr(BA)=tr(AB) — a basic trace property

Note: we do not get BA=Im. If A is n×m and B is m×n with m>n, then BA is an m×m projection of rank n<m. It projects Rm onto an n-dimensional subspace.

Problem 2: Projection ⇔ Disjoint Column Spaces (13 min) ​

True or False: For any square matrix P, the conditions P2=P and Col(I−P)∩Col(P)={0} are equivalent.

(a) Forward direction (⇒): Assume P2=P. Show Col(I−P)∩Col(P)={0}.

Solution

Let v∈Col(P)∩Col(I−P).

Since v∈Col(P): write v=Pw for some w. Then:

Pv=P2w=Pw=v

Since v∈Col(I−P): write v=(I−P)u for some u. Then:

Pv=P(I−P)u=(P−P2)u=0

From both: v=Pv=0. ◻

(b) Backward direction (⇐): Assume Col(I−P)∩Col(P)={0}. Show P2=P.

Hint: Consider the vector Px−P2x for any x. Show it belongs to both Col(P) and Col(I−P).

Solution

For any x, consider Px−P2x.

It belongs to Col(P): Both Px and P2x=P(Px) are in Col(P) (column space is a subspace, so closed under subtraction). Hence Px−P2x∈Col(P).

It belongs to Col(I−P): Write it as (I−P)(Px). This is clearly in Col(I−P).

By assumption, Col(P)∩Col(I−P)={0}, so:

Px−P2x=0for all x

Therefore P2=P. ◻

Attention

Why This Characterization Matters

The condition "P2=P" is algebraic. The condition "Col(I−P)∩Col(P)={0}" is geometric — it says the "floor" and the "sunlight direction" don't overlap.

This equivalence tells us: a matrix is a projection if and only if its floor and sunlight direction are disjoint. The "P2=P" equation is just the algebraic encoding of this geometric fact.

Part B: Block Cross-Filling (25 min) ​

The Main Idea ​

In rank-1 cross-filling, picking one row and one column determines a rank-1 piece: column × row ÷ pivot. What happens when we pick k rows and k columns at once? The next problems answer this — starting from a puzzle.

Sub-topic 1: The Matrix Formula — Rank-k Piece (10 min) ​

Problem 3: Complete the Matrix ​

The first two rows and first two columns of this 4×4 matrix are given, but the bottom-right block is hidden:

A=(2155124710??01??)

You are told that A has rank 2. Find the four missing entries.

(a) Since rank(A)=2, row 3 must be a linear combination of rows 1 and 2. Use columns 1 and 2 (which you can see) to find the coefficients, then fill in the missing entries of row 3.

Solution

row3=α⋅row1+β⋅row2. At columns 1 and 2:

2α+β=1,α+2β=0

Solving: α=23,β=−13.

Row 3 =23(2,1,5,5)−13(1,2,4,7)=(1,0,2,1).

(b) Do the same for row 4.

Solution

2α+β=0,α+2β=1

Solving: α=−13,β=23.

Row 4 =−13(2,1,5,5)+23(1,2,4,7)=(0,1,1,3).

Attention

What you just did is block cross-filling.

Name the three pieces:

  • Pivot block P=(2112) — the 2×2 intersection of selected rows and columns
  • Column block U=(21121001) — all rows at the selected columns (4×2)
  • Row block V=(21551247) — the selected rows, all columns (2×4)

The completed matrix is A=U⋅P−1⋅V — the rank-k piece.

This is the block version of "column × row ÷ pivot": the scalar pivot p is replaced by the pivot block P, and ÷p becomes ×P−1.

Sub-topic 2: The Entry Formula (15 min) ​

The matrix formula UP−1V builds the entire rank-k piece at once. But sometimes you only need one entry. In rank-1 cross-filling, a single entry is ui⋅vj/p. Is there a block version?

Problem 4: Complete the Block Matrix ​

A 6×6 matrix is partitioned into 2×2 blocks. The first row of blocks and the first column of blocks are known, but the four interior blocks are hidden:

M=(A11A12A13A21??A31??)

where each Aij is 2×2 and A11 is invertible.

Suppose M has rank 2. Express each ? in terms of the known blocks.

Hint: Apply the same idea as Problem 3, but with block "rows" and block "columns."

Solution

Same method as Problem 3, at the block level. For block position (i,j):

block (i,j)=Ai1⋅A11−1⋅A1j
Col 1Col 2Col 3
Row 1A11A12A13
Row 2A21A21A11−1A12A21A11−1A13
Row 3A31A31A11−1A12A31A11−1A13

This is the entry formula for block cross-filling:

(rank-k piece)ij=uiT⋅P−1⋅vj

where ui is row i's entries at the selected columns (a k-vector) and vj is column j's entries at the selected rows (a k-vector). The block-letter version makes the pattern visible: row-side × pivot-inverse × column-side.

Problem 5: Find the Missing Entries ​

The first two rows and first two columns are given. The rest is hidden:

M=(11302102140321????13????30????12????)

Suppose M has rank 2. Select the first 2 rows and columns as pivot.

(a) Find m3,4 using the entry formula.

Solution

P=(1102), P−1=(1−12012).

u3=(21), v4=(04).

m3,4=u3TP−1v4=(21)(1−12012)(04)=−2

(b) Find m5,6.

Solution

u5=(30), v6=(13).

m5,6=u5TP−1v6=(30)(1−12012)(13)=−32

(c) Fill in all 16 missing entries at once using the block formula.

Solution

Partition M=(PRC?) where C=(21133012), R=(30211403).

Since rank =2, the missing block is CP−1R:

CP−1=(2−12113−32112),CP−1R=(112−24−124424152−66−32722252)

Problem 6: The Remainder ​

Partition any matrix into four blocks with an invertible k×k pivot block:

A=(PRCD)

(a) For which D does A have rank k?

Solution

From Problems 3–5: if A has rank k, the bottom-right block is completely determined — it must equal CP−1R. So:

A has rank k⟺D=CP−1R

(b) If D≠CP−1R, what is the remainder after cross-filling with pivot block P?

Solution

The rank-k piece predicts D as CP−1R. The remainder is:

D−CP−1R

This is the Schur complement of P in A — the block version of d−cr/p from rank-1 cross-filling.

The entry formula for the remainder: remainderij=aij−uiT⋅P−1⋅vj.

Attention

Block Cross-Filling: Two Formulas

Rank-1Block (rank-k)
Pivotnumber pk×k matrix P
Columnvector u (n×1)block U (n×k)
Rowvector vT (1×n)block V (k×n)
Matrix formula1p⋅u⋅vTU⋅P−1⋅V
Entry formulaui⋅vjpuiT⋅P−1⋅vj
Remainder entryaij−uivjpaij−uiTP−1vj

In every formula, 1p is replaced by P−1, scalar multiplication becomes matrix multiplication, and the structure is otherwise identical.

Applications (optional, if time permits) ​

Problem 7: Block Cross-Filling for the Inverse — from Lecture 10 ​

Recall the Lecture 10 trick: to cross-fill A−1 without computing it, form the block matrix

M=(AII0)

and cross-fill with pivots in A.

(a) Using the block cross-filling formula, do this in a single step: treat A as the entire pivot block. What is the Schur complement?

Solution

Partition M=(PRCD) where P=A, R=I, C=I, D=0.

The Schur complement is:

D−CP−1R=0−I⋅A−1⋅I=−A−1

So the remainder is (000−A−1).

Since M=(AIIA−1) has rank r (we proved this in Lecture 10), the remainder must be zero when cross-filling completes. But this block formula shows the bottom-right directly reveals −A−1, whether we do the cross-filling in one block step or r individual steps.

(b) Use the entry formula to see what happens at a specific position. What is the (i,j) entry of the bottom-right remainder?

Solution

For position (i,j) in the bottom-right block: ui = column i of I = ei, and vj = row j of I = ej. So:

remainderij=0−eiT⋅A−1⋅ej=−(A−1)ij

Each entry of A−1 appears individually in the remainder — this is exactly what Lecture 10 showed step by step: each rank-1 cross-filling step reveals one "slice" of A−1, and the entry formula tells you which entries of the slice are determined by which pivot column and row.

Problem 8: Block Cross-Filling for the Product — from Lecture 12 ​

In Lecture 12, we proved det(AB)=det(A)det(B) by forming the block matrix

M=(A0IB)

and cross-filling with pivots in I.

(a) Using block cross-filling: treat I (bottom-left) as the pivot block. What is the Schur complement?

Solution

We select the n rows and n columns where I sits. The partition is:

  • Pivot block P=I (bottom rows, left columns)
  • C=A (top rows, left columns)
  • R=B (bottom rows, right columns)
  • D=0 (top rows, right columns)

Schur complement: D−CP−1R=0−A⋅I−1⋅B=−AB.

So the remainder is (0−AB00).

This is precisely what Lecture 12 showed: cross-filling the I block makes AB appear passively in the top-right!

(b) Use the entry formula to see how AB is built entry by entry.

Solution

For position (i,j) in the top-right block: ui = row i of A (the top rows at the left columns) and vj = column j of B (the bottom rows at the right columns). Since P=I:

rank-n pieceij=uiT⋅I−1⋅vj=(row i of A)⋅(column j of B)=(AB)ij

Each entry of AB appears as a single entry-formula computation! This is the block cross-filling perspective on matrix multiplication itself: the product AB is what you get when I is the pivot block and A, B are the column and row blocks.

(c) Now use the determinant of the block triangular matrix to conclude:

Solution

M=(A0IB) is block lower triangular.

By the block triangular determinant theorem:

det(M)=det(A)⋅det(B)

But cross-filling M produces pivots from I (all equal to 1) and then pivots from −AB (in the remainder). So:

det(M)=det(AB)(after accounting for signs)

Combining: det(A)det(B)=det(AB). ◻

(TA note: The full sign analysis using zigzag loops was done in Lecture 12. The point here is that students see the block cross-filling viewpoint — the I block acts as the pivot, the passive block reveals AB, and the block triangular structure gives the determinant factorization.)

Attention

Two Applications of One Idea

Lecture 10: InverseLecture 12: Product
Block matrix(AII0)(A0IB)
Pivot blockAI
Passive blockbottom-right (=0)top-right (=0)
What appears (matrix formula)−A−1−AB
What appears (entry formula)−(A−1)ij=−eiTA−1ej(AB)ij=aiT⋅bj

Common pattern: Place the "known" block at the pivot position. The "unknown" block (inverse, product) materializes in the passive position — visible either as a whole matrix (Schur complement) or entry by entry (entry formula).

TA Notes ​

Time allocation ​

PartTopicSuggested TimePriority
AProblem 1 (AB=In⇒rank(BA)=n)12 minCore
AProblem 2 (P2=P⇔ disjoint columns)13 minCore
B1Problem 3 (complete a rank-2 matrix — discover the matrix formula)10 minCore
B2Problem 4 (complete a block matrix — discover the entry formula)5 minCore
B2Problem 5 (find missing entries of a 6×6 matrix)5 minCore
B2Problem 6 (remainder — for which D is it rank k?)3 minCore
AppsProblems 7–8 (Lecture 10/12 applications)bonusIf time permits

Suggested flow ​

  1. Start with Problem 1: Students just took the exam, so this is a natural debrief. Walk through (a)–(b) quickly, then let students try (c). The one-line proof is satisfying — emphasize that three separate ideas (algebra, projection theory, trace) combine cleanly.

  2. Problem 2: This was the hardest True/False on the exam. The forward direction (a) is mechanical. For the backward direction (b), give the hint and let students struggle for 2–3 minutes before revealing. The key trick is to show Px−P2x lives in both subspaces.

  3. Transition to Part B: "Both of these problems used ideas we've built up. Now let's look at a technique that connects two different lectures — Lecture 10 (inverse) and Lecture 12 (determinant). The common thread is block cross-filling."

  4. Problem 3 — the key moment: Put the 4×4 matrix with the missing block on the board. Ask: "This has rank 2 — what are the four missing numbers?" Give students 2–3 minutes. Some will try column reduction, others will guess. The insight is: rank 2 means row 3 is a combination of rows 1 and 2, and you can find the coefficients from the visible columns. After the class solves it, reveal the attention box: what they just did is UP−1V.

  5. Problem 4 — abstract block version: Now put the 3×3 block matrix with hidden blocks on the board. "Same game, but with block letters." Students should be faster now — they apply the same logic at the block level and discover Ai1A11−1A1j.

  6. Problem 5 — numerical drill: The 6×6 matrix. Students practice the entry formula on specific entries (a, b) before filling the whole matrix (c). This shows the computational power: any single entry without building the entire matrix.

  7. Problem 6 — remainder: General (PRCD) partition. "For which D is this rank k?" Answer: D=CP−1R. "If not?" Remainder =D−CP−1R (the Schur complement). Quick and conceptual — builds on everything they've done.

  8. Applications (Problems 7–8): If time permits, show how Lectures 10 and 12 are both instances of this framework.

Common mistakes ​

  • Problem 1: Students may try to prove BA=Im (which is false when m≠n). Emphasize: we get rank=n, not invertibility.
  • Problem 2(b): Students may try to show Px−P2x∈Col(P) by writing it as P(x−Px). This gives Col(P) membership but the other inclusion is trickier. The key observation is Px−P2x=(I−P)(Px) for Col(I−P) membership.
  • Problem 3: Some students may try to solve a 4×4 system or do column reduction. Redirect: "You know it's rank 2 — what does that tell you about each row?"
  • Entry formula confusion: Students may confuse ui (row i of the column block U, a k-vector) with ui (the i-th entry of a column vector). Emphasize: in rank-1, each "entry" of the column is a number; in block cross-filling, each "entry" of the column block is a k-vector.
  • Problem 6: Students may struggle to see why D=CP−1R is the right condition. Connect back: "In Problem 3, the missing block had to be CP−1R — that's the only D making rank =k."

If time is short ​

Focus on Problems 3 and 4 (discover matrix formula, then entry formula). Skip Problem 5 (students can practice on their own) and abbreviate Problem 6 to just part (a). The applications (Problems 7–8) can be assigned as reading.
